title: Investigating Reproducibility Challenges in LLM Bugfixing on the HumanEvalFix Benchmark
reason: The paper investigates reproducibility challenges in the context of using large language models (LLMs) for automatic bugfixing on the HumanEvalFix benchmark. This is relevant to the desired topic of Machine Learning for Code, as it addresses the application of LLMs to a specific coding task (bugfixing) and discusses the impact of various evaluation settings on benchmark performance. The paper also explores discrepancies in reported results of LLMs, which is important for understanding the reliability and comparability of ML solutions in software engineering.

title: Correctness Assessment of Code Generated by Large Language Models Using Internal Representations
reason: The paper introduces a novel framework, OPENIA, which uses a white-box approach to assess the correctness of code generated by Large Language Models (LLMs). This framework leverages internal representations of LLMs during code generation, which is directly related to the use of machine learning in coding tasks. The paper also provides a systematic analysis of internal signals from LLMs and demonstrates the framework's effectiveness across various benchmarks and scenarios, making it relevant to the topic of Machine Learning For Code.

title: RECoRD: A Multi-Agent LLM Framework for Reverse Engineering Codebase to Relational Diagram
reason: The paper presents a novel framework, RECoRD, which leverages large language models for the task of reverse engineering codebases into relational diagrams. This is directly related to the use of machine learning (specifically large language models) to solve coding tasks, making it relevant to the desired topic. The paper introduces a new method that automates the extraction of causal dependencies from code, thus contributing to the field of machine learning for code. It is not a survey or literature review, and it introduces a significant new approach in the area of interest.

title: Autopresent: Designing structured visuals from scratch
reason: The paper introduces a new approach called AUTOPRESENT for automated slide generation from natural language instructions using machine learning, specifically leveraging large language models (LLMs) for program generation. This is directly relevant to the topic of Machine Learning for Code, as it presents a novel ML-based solution for a coding task (generating presentation slides) and introduces SLIDES BENCH, a benchmark for evaluating such tasks. The paper fits within the scope of proposed solutions for coding tasks using ML and provides a new benchmark, which aligns with the desired topic.

title: Di-bench: Benchmarking large language models on dependency inference with testable repositories at scale
reason: The paper introduces DI-BENCH, a new benchmark specifically designed to evaluate large language models' (LLMs) capability in dependency inference for software repositories. This is directly relevant to the topic of 'Machine Learning For Code' as it focuses on a significant aspect of coding tasks using ML, namely the inference of dependencies which is crucial for automated software synthesis. Additionally, the paper provides detailed experiments and analysis on the performance of various LLMs, making it a valuable contribution to the study of ML solutions in software engineering.

title: Syncmind: Measuring agent out-of-sync recovery in collaborative software engineering
reason: The paper introduces a new framework, SyncMind, for addressing the out-of-sync problem faced by AI agents in collaborative software engineering. It also presents a benchmark, SyncBench, which is a new dataset that features agent out-of-sync scenarios derived from real-world GitHub repositories. The work is highly relevant to the topic of Machine Learning for Code as it examines the capabilities and limitations of large language model agents in collaborative software engineering, providing insights into the use and impact of ML solutions in this domain.

title: SWE-Dev: Building Software Engineering Agents with Training and Inference Scaling
reason: The paper introduces SWE-Dev, a novel software engineering agent built upon large language models, specifically designed to handle complex software engineering tasks. The paper presents a new method for training and inference scaling, introduces a new dataset, and provides experimental results showing its effectiveness on a relevant benchmark (SWE-bench-Verified). This is directly related to the topic of using machine learning for coding tasks and benchmarks in software engineering.

title: Coding Agents with Multimodal Browsing are Generalist Problem Solvers
reason: The paper presents a new method, OpenHands-Versa, which is a generalist AI agent designed to solve diverse coding tasks using machine learning. It introduces and evaluates the agent on software engineering benchmarks, specifically SWE-Bench Multimodal, demonstrating its effectiveness in coding tasks. This is directly relevant to the topic of machine learning solutions for coding tasks and provides empirical results on benchmarks relevant to software engineering.

title: CodeV: Issue Resolving with Visual Data
reason: The paper introduces a novel approach, CODEV, which leverages visual data to enhance the issue-resolving capabilities of large language models (LLMs) in the context of software engineering. It proposes a new method specifically addressing the challenge of resolving GitHub issues by incorporating visual data, which is relevant to the desired topic of Machine Learning for Code. Additionally, the paper introduces a new benchmark, Visual SWE-bench, designed to evaluate the performance of LLMs in resolving visual GitHub issues. These contributions are directly related to the application of machine learning in software engineering, making the paper relevant to the topic.

title: ELABORATION: A Comprehensive Benchmark on Human-LLM Competitive Programming
reason: The paper introduces ELABORATION, a novel benchmark specifically designed for evaluating human-LLM collaboration in competitive programming. It includes a new dataset, ELABORATIONSET, and provides detailed evaluations and methodologies for assessing human-LLM interaction in solving coding tasks. This aligns with the desired topic of studies on the use and impact of machine learning solutions in software engineering, particularly in the context of competitive programming.
